informal: If $H$ is the unique subgroup of a given order in a group $G$ prove $H$ is characteristic in $G$.
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_4_4_7 {G : Type*} [Group G] {H : Subgroup G} [Fintype H]
  (hH : ∀ (K : Subgroup G) (fK : Fintype K), card H = @card K fK → H = K) :
  H.Characteristic
reasoning: The informal statement says: if H is the unique subgroup of G of its (given) order, then H is characteristic. The Lean signature assumes [Fintype H] (so H has a finite order, matching the implicit reading of "order"), and the hypothesis hH states that any subgroup K with the same cardinality as H equals H — exactly the uniqueness condition. The conclusion H.Characteristic matches "H is characteristic in G". Restricting K to those with a Fintype instance is not a real restriction, because any K with the same cardinality as the finite H must itself be finite (and Fintype is a subsingleton). Hence the encoding is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
